speaker: interviewee
text: Then I worked for some time in a factory that's far from the city, but still in Budapest. This was a factory of-- I don't for [? siphon ?] bottles, the little oxygen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, soda. This was a very strenuous work, 10 hours a day-- but not an eight hour day, but 10 hours. My girlfriend, who introduced us with Jan, she also had papers. And she also worked there. And very soon, the other workers-- we worked with woman only, with woman there-- they got suspicious. Because they had a very vulgar talk. And we didn't talk this way. So this was the first thing. Then, it was strange to them that I had my lunch in a white paper and not in a, a journal paper--

speaker: interviewer
text: Newsprint.

speaker: interviewee
text: Newspaper, not in a newspaper-- that I washed my hands before I started to eat lunch. They didn't. So all this was very strange. And they started to talk. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they suspect you may be Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, maybe they suspected. They did-- I, I don't remember ever hearing anything like that. But they surely suspected. And so after a while, the whole place became very hot. And, and it was not safe. So I didn't go there anymore. Then I worked--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to change the way you looked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a young girl, I had very dark hair, almost black. And after the pastry shop episode, I bleached my hair. I went to hairdresser. And I had blonde. I became blonde. So it was a little bit better, a little bit better. But I  still looked Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some friends of ours who lived also with false papers, they told Jan that why is he going with me? This is dangerous, because I look so Jewish. So he said, never mind. [LAUGHS] Then I worked in a paper bag shop. There were-- When would that be, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was all in, in the summer of 1944-- the summer of 1944. There in the morning, we had to make the paper bags. And in the afternoon, I had to go to different stores to sell it. And there was one epi-- I was not very good at that. I went into a store. And I asked the, the proprietor--

speaker: interviewer
text: The manager, the owner.

speaker: interviewee
text: The manager, yes, whether he wants to buy some paper bags. And he said, look, miss, a Jew would be doing this way. He would look it up my name outside. And he would come in. Hello, Mr. Brown. How are you doing today? We have excellent paper bags for you. So that's the way you have to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was again such a memorable day, August 24, 1944. This was Jan's 24th birthday. And we went to a restaurant for lunch. Yeah, it was more like a milk bar or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were sitting with our lunch. And suddenly at the door, three men came in, in two piece suit and hats-- fedoras. And we immediately knew that these are detectives. And Jan told me, try to get out. Because they were standing at the door. Said to try to get out. And I told him, I'm not going without you. Either I stay with you, or we are going together. He said, don't be crazy. We have exactly the same papers. In the moment they put it next to each other, we are both finished. And I have to pay, because this would be very strange, suspicious, running out without paying. Go. And then we said at what street and where should I wait. He will come after me. So I went to the door and put my hand on the shoulder of one of these detectives. And I said, excuse me. And he said, oh, yes, miss. Please, go ahead. And I was out. And I went to that street where we talked-- what we arranged. And I waited two hours. I was 100% sure that they took Jan. Finally, finally after two hours, he came from a completely different direction. He said that first of all, he had to wait a long time because the restaurant was full, so until the waiter came and he could pay. And then he didn't know whether these people are not following me. So he didn't want to take the same route. So he went half the city around and around until finally, he got there. So this was-- I think during those two hours, I decided that I will marry him. It's a very important two hours. Then on October 15, 1944, the Hungarian Arrow Cross Party, which was a Hungarian variant of the swastika, the Hungarian fascists, they, they took over. The Germans arrested the Regent Horthy and his son. And the fascists took over. And then it started in earnest. It became an extremely, extremely dangerous time. Then they started to build the ghetto in Budapest. The thing was that the Germans didn't have time any more to deport the Jews from Budapest, because the Soviet army was too close. So they put them in ghettos. The men and some of the woman were-- went also to labor camps. But there was always a possibility to take everybody on foot to Austria. Austria was not yet occupied or liberated, whatever. [LAUGHS] So this, this was a terrible time. At that time I didn't work already, because it was really dangerous to even, to go out in the street. And on December the 7th, I went in the morning to buy something. And there was a police raid. I fell in. And they asked my papers. And they asked questions and so on. And they took me with several other men and women. They put me in jail and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the Hungarian jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Hungarian jail with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hun-- the Hungarian police. I was very lucky in that, because I must say that the police was not so bad. They were relatively, relatively civil. To get to the hands of the Arrow Cross Party people or to the Germans, this would be catastrophic, of course. But the Hungarian police was not so terribly bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they suspect you had false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. They recognized. And they, they, they knew, of course, that I am Jewish. And they put us in jail. The first few days, we didn't get anything to eat. And some people started to faint already from hunger. And one policeman let his wife to cook a huge pan of some cabbage soup or something and at evening brought it in and gave us to eat. I was very, very weak of the hunger. And this policeman hold my head and with a spoon, fed me from the soup. So this must be said, because the whole Hungary was terrible during the war. And they were fascists and everything. But there were people who dared to be different. And if not these people, we wouldn't be alive. Because we did what we did. But we always needed some help. We always needed some people. There was also the, the lady where I found a bed for myself in Budapest. She was a single woman, a quite simple, uneducated woman. After several months that I lived there, I told her my true identity. This was the time when they started to issue food rationing. And we couldn't falsificate the, the rationing. It was impossible. So by that time, I knew her already. And I knew her opinion. So I told her. And she was extremely, extremely nice. When I was in prison, she came to the prison. And she wanted to bring me some clean underwear and some food. And they didn't let her in. They said that these people cannot have visitors. And she started to yell and everything. They sent her away. But this was very dangerous. This was very dangerous she did that. And later on, we had some acquaintances who lived at first in Vienna. And then a woman with a little girl, 10-years-old girl, they came to Budapest. The woman was-- they were Jews. The woman was taken to a labor camp. And this 10-years-old girl remained alone. And I took her. And I asked my landlady whether I can bring this girl here. And she said, of course. We must save her. She took--

speaker: interviewer
text: So she risked herself.

speaker: interviewee
text: She took this girl into her own bed, because she didn't have anything else, just one bed where  she slept and a couch where I slept. She took this girl into her bed. And she slept with her. And she fed her during the end, during the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- do you remember your-- the woman's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes-- Rose Balazs, R-O-S-E B-A-L-A-Z-S. She doesn't live anymore. But after the war, of course, we visited her. And I must also tell, I didn't have any money. She didn't do it for money. She couldn't expect any money from anybody. She did just out of--

speaker: interviewer
text: The goodness of her heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of her human feeling, human feeling. So I was in, in prison. And after a few days, Jan-- yeah, no, there was one episode I must say. When I was taken to the prison, ev-everybody was stripped and checked. I had a blouse. And here was a little pocket. And I had with me a picture of my mother. And I folded it and put it in that pocket. And when it was my turn to strip, then I took off the blouse already so the woman who did the searching, she didn't notice that I have a picture in my pocket. So this picture was with me. After a few days, I was, in evening, I was standing in the door of our cell. And one of the-- what is it called-- one of the policemen, one of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guard, yeah, one of the guard came. And he came to me. And he started to touch me. And he opened my blouse. And he touched my breast. And then he said, what do you have here? And I said, that's my mother's picture. He took away his hand and said, ah, go. I knew at that moment that my mother, who didn't live at that time anymore, but she saved me from that. After a few days, I asked a policeman-- on this picture on the other side, I wrote a few words to my father that I am [INAUDIBLE] here. And if he can, he should help me. I must also tell that I didn't tell this that after I left from the ghetto, just a fe-- two or three days after that, my father and also my cousin were taken to labor camps. So my mother and grandmother stayed there. And the beginning of June, the first days of June, they were deported to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody from Miskolc was deported to Auschwitz. And some people came back. Because my mother had a very severe heat condition and my grandmother was old, they went with some hospital train. And these people were immediately sent, to to gas. I talked to some friends of ours who came back. And this woman told me that they saw my mother and grandmother going towards the gas chambers. So my father was in an Hungarian labor camp, my cousin too, but not together. They were completely different places. My father was there till the fall of '44. And then, the German and the Hungarians, together with them, took these people on foot to Austria. Naturally, many of these people didn't survive because this was a terrible march. My father managed to escape in Bu-- when they were near to Budapest. He managed to escape. And he was hiding in Budapest. Now, there where I think Jan talked about [? Raul ?] [? Vallenberg. ?] They issued- the Swedish embassy and also the Swiss embassy-- they issued some papers. They designated some houses, which were so called-- I don't know how to tell this-- guarded houses. Yes. There was a house which used to be a warehouse for glasses. It was called the glass house. So they somehow took out the glasses. And 2,000 Jews were crammed in-- among them, my father. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about  it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I know about it. I didn't want to go in that. I never wanted to be in a place crammed together. So I sent to this address this young policeman with my mother's picture and the written text. So my father knew. And Jan sometimes went to visit my father. So they were in contact. And Jan immediately started to work on that. He managed to find connection to the prison doctor, managed to get money from the Zionist organization to give to the doctor. And I got the message when we got-- we got already food that time. And when somebody brought out food, they told me the next day, you should go to the doctor. You should ask to be taken to the doctor, because you have big pain in your tummy. I said, appendicitis.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did that. And the doctor examined me and said, yes. It is appendicitis and must be operated immediately. And a policeman took me to a-- out from the prison to a Jewish hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the only Jewish hospital with which I had to ride to be outside the ghetto. And of course, again, this was a place where hundreds and hundreds were crammed in. I, I got a place half a bed. Another woman was in this bed. This was a horrible place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the evening, the doctor of the hospital came in. And he said, everybody just be quiet. There won't be any raid from the Arrow Cross people. But in case there will be a raid, so everybody knows what is their illness and so on, and so on. So I said, that's not the place for me. I am not staying here. Next day-- luckily at night, there was no raid-- and the next day there was, there were visitation hours. And after the visitation hours ended, I took my coat. And I went out with the visitors.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to the place where Jan stayed with his Polish papers. He had there an iron bed in a kitchen with a curtain in front of it. And again, the landlady was a very simple woman, but an extremely courageous and good woman. Her husband tended to be a fascist.

speaker: interviewee
text: So actually, she had to hide me from her husband. They knew Jan that he's Polish, so this was all right. But I was there sort of just visiting or so. But she gave me food, which was very precious at that time. And again, I didn't have money. So I was there about three days until Jan managed to get new papers for me. Then-- What was your name in the new papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was again Eva, E-V-A, Karcag, K-A-R-C-A-G. And Jan took me to an apartment where he used to live. I think they also knew him as Polish. So the landlady took me in. We said that the house where I stayed, it was bombed, which was an everyday occurrence. It was nothing special. Everybody could believe that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed there. This was already before Christmas, 1944. The war raged on, really on the streets of Budapest. This was the front. The Russians were already nearly in the city, almost in the city around. And the Germans were fighting their last-- to their last men.

speaker: interviewee
text: After Christmas, I think, or after New Year, after New Year, the bombardment was so terrible that we couldn't stay in the apartment. This was, I think, on the third or fourth floor. And everybody had to go down to the cellar, to the shelter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this was a big apartment house. But still, everybody knew his neighbor. And I was a new face. So everybody asked, who is she? Who is she? So my landlady got cold feet.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't blame her. She had an old mother and a young son. And she said I must go. She is very sorry, but she cannot keep me there. This was already in the evening. And there was a curfew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the evening I couldn't, go anywhere. I went to a co-- to the coal cellar, which was next to the shelter. And I was sitting in this coal cellar the whole night. And in the morning when we were allowed to go at the street, I went to Jan again, to this apartment where he was Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Normally, this is about a 15, 20 minutes walk. It took me more than two hours. I went on the street. No, no people-- absolutely nobody was on the street. There were fight. This was the front. I got a German tank-- a Tiger what it was called-- came so close that I could just hardly jump away. Then I went. I had to cross the street somewhere. And in the middle of the street, there was a dead man with the hole in his head and a piece of paper. This is the fate of one who helps the Jews. So I had to jump over this cadaver and continue. But I always could go from one house to another and then wait and then again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, I got to Jan, that here I am. I have nowhere to stay. So I couldn't stay there with him because of the man. So he took me to another place where he had a tiny little room.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this was-- yes, this was his Hungarian place. They knew me already there. Because during-- while he was staying there, I used to come. I was his bride-- Eva, his bride. By that time, everybody was in the shelter. So the landlady said, OK, stay here in the shelter. They had two kitchen chairs. And they said, we can't give you a mattress or a bed or anything. Try to lie down on these two chairs. I didn't have money. I didn't have anything to eat. Because normally, pe-people prepared something. They-- everybody had at least some dry legumes. And they cooked it everyday, but something. I didn't have absolutely anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes some people said, there is a little soup left. Do you want it, Eva? I say, oh, yes. Thank you. And I ate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was in the cellar a prostitute, a beautiful, beautiful woman-- not Jewish, of course. She had a bed. And during the day, she told me, come, lie down. Rest a bit on my bed. At night when nobody saw it, she gave me to eat. That's what I had to eat. In the, in the shelter, there were three Hungarian officers in their full, beautiful uniform. They were not brave enough to go out and fight. But they were brave enough to harass people. And one evening, these officers sat together with this prostitute.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, this Eva looks very suspicious. Isn't she Jewish? And this prostitute said, aren't  you ashamed of yourself? You are Hungarian officers. You cannot tell a Jew apart? If Eva is Jewish, I am the Pope in Rome. So they shut their mouths. And practically, she saved my life. Because even at the very end when it was absolutely clear that the Germans lost the war, even then, such people like these officers, they pulled out Jews from houses in Budapest who were there-- or like me-- and shot them on the street or hanged them on a tree, even the very last moment. Do you think she knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: She did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she knew. She knew. On January the 8th, 1944, when there was the most horrible bombardment and fighting and not a soul on the streets, only the tanks and the cannons, suddenly Jan appeared in our shelter with a big bread under his arm. He knew that I am without any means. And he had some money. And he bought some flour and paid a woman to bake a big bread. In Hungary, they bake this huge, round, big bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he brought the ba-- the bread for me. And then, of course, he had to make the trip once more back to his place. So it was really life-threatening. And only a crazy young boy in love can do such a thing. But as so many times, he again saved my life. So the first Russian soldiers came in January 18, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Very shortly after that, Jan came already. He was liberated two days earlier, because they fought for every street. And then immediately, we went to this glass house to find my father. So we found him. And-- What kind of shape was he in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was all right. He was, as always, the, the factotum. He was responsible for the feeding of these 2,000 people. Because all his life, he had to do something with ba-- my grandfather was a baker. So he had always been with bakers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he managed to somehow to get flour in Budapest. And they made this like, I don't know. What should I say? Like  a pizza or like some dough, some-- and this-- Flat bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And this was what these people were eating. So he was terribly busy during the war time. He didn't have time to think of himself. To stay in Budapest, it was very insecure. Fist of all, January the 18th, only Pest was liberated. This is a twin city on the two sides of the Danube. Buda-- the Germans retreated to Buda and fought-- and the Soviets, too, naturally. And Buda was liberated, I think, almost a month later, sometimes towards the end of February. But it was very unsure to stay in Budapest, because there were cities in Hungary which changed the government several times. The Germans fought back, then the Soviets fought back. Then again, the Germans came back. So it was very, very insecure. And Jan said that just out from here as soon as possible. So the two of us--
